# Group Definitions and Transition Analysis

<!--
This chapter defines the clinical groups used throughout the study 
and motivates why each comparison is scientifically relevant.
-->

## Transition Framework

<!--
Explain how transitions are derived from longitudinal NACC visits:
- Comparing diagnosis at visit N with diagnosis at visit N+1
- Or comparing first visit diagnosis with last visit diagnosis
- How NACCUDSD or similar variables encode diagnostic status
-->

```{python}
#| label: transition-matrix

# Transition matrix: from-diagnosis (rows) × to-diagnosis (columns)
# Visualize as heatmap or Sankey diagram
```

## Early Detection: NL → NL vs. NL → MCI {#sec-early-detection}

<!--
Research question: What distinguishes individuals who remain cognitively 
normal from those who develop MCI?

Clinical relevance: Identifying at-risk individuals before symptom onset 
enables earlier intervention and clinical trial enrollment.
-->

```{python}
#| label: early-detection-groups

# Sample sizes, demographic comparison
# Baseline feature distributions for both groups
```

## Disease Progression: NL → MCI, MCI → AD, NL → AD {#sec-progression}

<!--
Research question: Which features predict the speed and trajectory 
of cognitive decline?

Clinical relevance: Understanding progression markers helps 
stratify patients by risk severity.
-->

```{python}
#| label: progression-groups

# Sample sizes per progression path
# Compare feature profiles across progression types
```

## Disease Regression: MCI → MCI vs. MCI → NL {#sec-regression}

<!--
Research question: What characterizes individuals whose cognitive 
status improves or reverts to normal?

Clinical relevance: Understanding reversibility may reveal modifiable 
risk factors or identify misdiagnosed cases.
-->

```{python}
#| label: regression-groups

# Sample sizes
# Feature comparison: stable MCI vs. reverters
# Discussion of potential explanations (practice effects, 
#   depression resolution, misdiagnosis)
```
